speaker: interviewer
text: And it's December 1, 1995. Could you tell me a little bit about life in-- when you were in your twenties?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In my 20s, I remember I went for a New Year to the other city, Koprivnica. K-O-P-R-I-V-N-I-C-A-- Koprivnica. And I remember, uh, it was a big, uh, New Year party. And I saw, uh-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] next table I saw three young men sitting there. And one was just watching me all the time, and I felt a little bit uncomfortable. But he just stared at me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when the dancing starts, he sent his friend to dance with me. And he says-- he asked me who I am, or from where I am from. And he says, you know, my friend, he fell in love with you. I says, how could he fell in love with me? He doesn't know me. He saw me now at first-- he said, he's just crazy about you, he would like to meet you, and can I introduce you to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, I don't know. Because I was in love with other guy-- [LAUGHS]-- in Slatina a little bit. Nothing serious, but I liked him very much. I says, no, I don't think I want to meet him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And next day, I got roses at my aunt's house from him. And, uh, his friend called. He says, how about going to movie? I would like to take to movie. Would you like to go? I says, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went to movie, and this other guy was there, so he introduced me. And he asking me to get married right away-- just like that. And I don't know what possessed me. I says, OK. I says, OK. And I didn't even know him too much. But I really wanted to go from home. I know this guy, I-- never would come anything serious between us for many reasons. I says, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after three days I went home. I says-- I came home, and big bunch of roses were waiting already. And my mother said, now what's going on? I says, mother, I am engaged. She says, what? I says, yes, I met a guy, and I am engaged. And he came, uh-- after couple days he came with his brother to our home to ask my hand of marriage. And my parents liked him very much. He was a nice man. And he had a very good business. He was very wealthy. And they were crazy about him, more than I was.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I-- I liked him. But I didn't really know him, because he want back to Koprivnica. He just came every weekend to see me. And that was going on, uh, two months. Because I met him in December. In February 20, we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, his name was Dory, D-O-R-Y. Dory Weiss, W-E-I-S-S-- Weiss. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember when they put veil on me. I started to all of a sudden cry. I says, oh my God, what am I doing? I don't love him. I like him, but I don't love him. But I couldn't disappoint anybody. And we got married. I had a beautiful wedding. And we went to honeymoon to, uh, Dubrovnik. It's a, uh-- on Adriatic Sea. And I'm embarrassed, but I was three days married, and I didn't let him touch me, you know? I was scared, and I was naive, young. And then he says, you know what? If you don't want to be married, go back to home. Because after all, I married you. I want you to be my wife. So I did. And after that was normal. But there was-- he was wonderful guy to me. But I felt something was wrong. I couldn't put my finger on what was wrong.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was married maybe six months. I got pregnant. And he didn't want me to have child. He just didn't want me-- I remember I cried. I says, I want children. No, I had to go and have abortion. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: After six months, again I got pregnant. And the same story-- he didn't-- but then I noticed that something is wrong with him. And his best friend was a physician, so I asked him. He says, don't you know that he's sick man? I says, what do you mean sick? He says, he has multiple sclerosis.

speaker: interviewee
text: I says no, I didn't know. He never said. I noticed something-- he never wanted to walk too much. He always hang to me, or if he have to go to walk a little bit further, we always went with the-- with the buggies, you know. But I didn't-- I didn't-- didn't know why. I thought he just likes to do that. And after second time I did, I said, oh my God. I was so unhappy. But I didn't say anything to my parents, to anybody. I just kept in me. I thought, I married him. That was my destiny. I would never dissolve my marriage, never would leave him because-- I got used to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because he was a good man, and I had everything. I married, I right away have a cook and a maid. I didn't have to do anything. He didn't let me out from his sight. I always had to go with him to his business, always with him, you know? And then, end of '40, I got again pregnant. I says, I don't want to have anymore abortion. I want this baby. And I went to Zagreb to a specialist. And I asked him, I says, is that hereditary? He says, not necessarily, no. I says, I don't want abortion. I want to have this baby. He says, yes, you can have it. So I came home from Zagreb, and he was so unhappy. He says, no, I don't want the baby. I don't want it. So he sent me to my parents, to Slatina, that I would do it there. But the doctor, he says, no, he won't do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I went to Osik, to other city to a sanatorium. And they, says yes, they can do it, but I have to be there for-- uh, in hospital. So I called him. He says, no, you come back. I already arranged that they're going to do it in Kopravnica. I came back. In the meantime, he got sick. And I just forget about it. Then it was too late to do it. And thank God. She was born-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in '41. In August '41 she was born-- beautiful little baby. And she was only couple weeks old when the German occupied. And they took me in concentration camp together, you know, all the Jews in this town-- my husband, my baby. They took us to Gospic. That was the first camp. We were there one month, and I was reunited with my parents. They were in other city, you know. But they came, my parents and my sister. In meantime, my older sister got married. And so we were all together for a month. Oh, gosh, they were crazy about my little baby. Her name was Mariam-- big eyes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I didn't have nothing to give her to eat anymore, you know? She was-- I don't know how she, uh, did. In beginning, somebody brought me some milk. I could give it to her.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then after month, they took us in other camp. That was Jaska, J-A-S-K-A. We were there, I don't know how many-- uh, almost thousand people already there from all over Yugoslavia. And we were there maybe three weeks. Then one day, they told us all to come out, women on one side and the men on other side. And they came with-- with, uh-- with, uh-- uh-- [NON-ENGLISH]-- with guns pointed on us and says, the men have to go in one line and go. They separated us. That was the worst moment on my life. Everybody was screaming, the women, because we couldn't even say goodbye to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember my father, he took, uh-- then my husband already was bad. He hardly could walk, you know? And my father took him by hand and then other-- my brother. And they went. We were looking how they went.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: It was horrible. And after about two weeks later, they took us in other camp, only women-- Kruscica. That was the worst. That was in Bosnia. Kruscica-- should I spell it? K-R-U-S-C-I-A, Kruscica.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were beaten there. We were hungry. Some of them were raped. We were in barracks without anything, you know-- no food, no water. Once in day, maybe, have potatoes, what we had. I didn't-- we were there one month. I didn't see the water. I couldn't wash my baby. She made everything in diaper, and I couldn't even wash, you know. She was already starting to cry-- all the time crying. And those Ustase-- they were Ustase, is like Nazis, you know, Croatian Ustase. They said they going to kill my baby. She cannot cry. So I remember my mother, my sisters-- all night we kept her mouth like that so they couldn't hear her crying. And one morning, she was so bad crying. And I went up from the barracks, and I begged them, uh, a little tea. Because our prisoners who were there, they cooked, you know. And he was Jewish, but he was very mean. And he went to Ustase. He says, I want tea for the baby. And Ustase came with-- with his gun. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he wanted to beat me. And my mother came, and she stood in front of me. And he wanted to hit my mother. I says, yeah, I'm going to kill you. You won't touch my mother. And I pushed my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then he started to beat me. And more he beat me, more I laughed. I was laughing like idiot, you know? And then somehow they took me from him in barrack. And then I started to cry. I remember that very well.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember how he treated, uh-- this camp was, uh, like a little burg. We were together, Serbian and Jewish together. They were about five barracks. In our barracks, they didn't, uh-- they started, but they didn't do anything. But the next barrack, they just came in, and they took the young girls, and in front of everybody, they-- they raped them. And we heard crying. And then in the night, we heard crying and yelling. It was a black house on the top where they kept Serbian prisoner. And they tortured them. And we heard them, uh, crying all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: We couldn't go to the toilet. They have a ladder, you know. We-- we-- we went out. But if you're very young, they raped us, so we couldn't go. If they were old, they beat them, you know? So we-- we had to do everything in-- in our barracks, you know? It was horrible. It was just horrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: Till one day, they said we are leaving. My-- my daughter was then three months old. And they took us again with the train, with the train for the animals, you know. They shut, and, you know-- and we never knew where we going. And they brought us through Zagreb. And they talk us-- tell us to take all the clothes. And naked, we had to go, uh, through the road there, uh, to the place where they, uh, disinfected us. We were full with, uh-- with, uh-- no-- louse.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was-- that-- that was our entertainment in camp. We did to each other, how many louse we-- [LAUGHS] Anyway, we had to be there. And American-- not American-- the Red Cross was there, the nurses and the doctor. And I saw them. I says, please, can you give me for my baby? She didn't eat anything. So they brought me-- the nurse brought me milk. And this baby took it like that. And in one gargle, she took it back right away out. I says, can you help me? She says, I try.

speaker: interviewee
text: They put us in the train for Loborgrad. It was before they took us for Auschwitz. And I was already-- the train and the baby-- all of a sudden, they call my name-- Zora and Mariam Weiss, get out from the train. We are taking you hospital. And I remember I didn't want to go. I says, I don't want to go without my mother and my sister. And my mother pushed me. She says, go. So we went. We-- they took us with ambulance to the hospital. They left baby there, and I went to my sister-in-law. She was still living in Zagreb. She wasn't taken away. And I went next day to hospital. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I says, I want to see my baby. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they brought it out. I took her. And I never forget. I still see her eyes-- big, black eyes. She just looked at me. She didn't smile. She didn't cry, nothing-- just looked in my eyes, you know? I could hold her for 10 minutes, then I left. And the next day I came. I asked for baby. He says, no, they cannot bring her. I says, why? No, they cannot bring her. I says, at least let me-- I begged them me see it. So they took me in the room. And she died in front of me. She was dying already.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And, uh, I remember when I went from hospital, I was not crying. I was screaming, you son of a-- what did you do to my baby? I didn't care if they gonna put me in the camp or whatever. I-- I was so unhappy.

speaker: interviewee
text: But days go on. Then they took me in hospital because I-- I don't know, was it breakdown or something. I was a couple of days there. [SIGHS] Then I didn't know where to go. My sister-in-law, she says, I am afraid to take you, because I know they are watching my house. So I went to sleep to some-- the Jewish organization still was there. And they put me to a house, where I slept by those people. I slept there three days. And then I said, no, tonight I won't sleep by you. I'm going to my sister-in-law. Next day when I wanted to go there, they were taking-- this night they were taking when I didn't sleep. They took them, so-- destiny. Three times happened I slept by some families, and each time I didn't sleep there, they were taken. Then I thought, oh, my God, I better go in Podravska Slatina to my grandparents, because they were still there. So I went to-- to them. And I was there couple of days. Then I says, oh, no. I won't stay there. I-- I didn't like it. I had a feeling that they won't be too long.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went back to Zagreb, where I have these friends, my two girlfriends, who were just wonderful. And then I stay one day with one, next day with the other friend. And then they took me in hospital because I got very sick. I had a, uh, female trouble, because, you know, three months-- baby was only three weeks after I had a baby and slept on the floor without anything. So I was a little sick. So they took me in hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was experience, too. One day, SS came in my room that was friend of mine from childhood who was always Nazi. I didn't know that. He was terribly in love always with me. And he came, and I was happy. I says, oh, a friend, you know, came to see me. And he says, Zora, I want to help you. I says, you can help me. Take me to Jasenovac, where my husband and all the men were. He says, OK, I'm going to take you. I come next day, and I'm going to take you. I was so excited. Next day he came. I says, I'm ready. He says, Zora, I cannot take you. He is not living anymore. Your-- your father, everybody was killed already. I remember I fainted. I couldn't believe it, you know. He did it in such a way, you know, without feelings. Oh, they are dead already. They were-- they were killed. Then I thought, oh, I better be afraid of-- of you. And I-- I escaped from the hospital to my girlfriend. And then, uh, I met friend of mine who was with me in concentration camp, who was taken from the train, too, because she had dysentery. And she was taken, she heard about me, that I am out, too. So we met. And we took a room together. We were there for, oh, about couple of months. We were afraid to go out, you know. But the friends brought us food there. And one day even I went to Novigrad, where my mother and sister in camp. I went to visit them. I took some food and took them. And when I came there-- that's the last time I saw my mother-- and my sister there with the gates, you know. And my mother said, go. Don't you ever come. Go. Because they are asking for you. They want you back in camp. Disappear.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I did. I disappeared. I was by my girlfriend, and I had some, uh-- some jewelry, what my girlfriend saved for me. And they sold it to have passport for, um, Adriatic Sea, where the Italian were, occupied by Italian. So I escaped there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there about three months, and I didn't have any money anymore, because I came with a couple hundred, uh, dinar. That would be maybe $50, you know, I came. And it was a man there, a family who was my, uh, father friend, very well-to-do man.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I came to him. I says, Uncle Alfred, can you help me? I don't have any more money. I cannot pay the rent and the food. He says, oh, yes. He says, I'm going to take the room for you, and you're going to be my mistress. When I heard that, I went home, and I wanted to make suicide. I-- I didn't succeed, because they find me in time. They pumped my stomach. And three days later, they took us in Italian camp. And I was the happiest person, because my worry was over. At least I didn't have worry, how can I pay my rent and the food?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took us-- that was in Krvavica in-- in Croatia. Italian put us in the camp. That was Italian camp, but no comparison with the other camp. They really saved our lives. I had-- we had barracks, but we had a nice, uh, you know, bath and, uh everything, the food. Then I met my second husband. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] before I met him, I-- I was like loner. I only played with the children. Whenever I saw the children, I wanted to play with the children. I was, at the time, a little bit maybe crazy. I don't know. But then when I met my husband, he was-- first of all, he was very handsome. He was so wonderful. And I fell, first time in my life, really, madly in love. And I didn't care, concentration camp or not. I was very, very happy. We were there for, uh, I don't know how many months. Then they transferred us in other concentration camp, still in Italian-Yugoslav zone on the RAB, R-A-B. And we were there till liberation. Uh, the partisan took over, and then we were liberated from the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then started our problem. Where to go? Some people went to fight with the partisan, and some people tried to escape to Italy or Switzerland. So we tried for Italy. But before we came there, we went only night with a little boat. We went over the night till we reached Vis-- V-I-S-- where the-- uh-- um-- Allies were there, English, uh, from England.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were there, I don't know, a couple days. Then partisan wanted to take my husband to fight. All men, they had to go to fight. And he says, I cannot go. I cannot leave my-- my-- uh, we were not then married yet, but he said 'my wife.'

speaker: interviewer
text: And they took examination, and they told him, he says, oh, look at this man. Put your hands like that. They says, Jesus is coming. Because his beard was, you know-- all of a sudden he was all white. And he put the beard-- he looked really bad. And he says, uh, you are, um-- [NON-ENGLISH] how would you say that? You are, uh-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] too-- too old-- too quick old, something like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says, you can go with your wife to Italy. So then they took us to the ship. We came to the body, Italy. That's how Italy, um-- the British were there. They gave us food there. And, uh, we were there only two days, and they put us in the ship. They wanted us to go to Africa-- all people who were survived to Africa. And we escaped from the, uh-- from the ship with some other friends. And we stayed in Italy. And that was the wonderful time we had. He had a very good job. He was working for, uh-- uh-- for Allied, as for interpreter and then later for Jewish organization. And I got right away job. I was interpreter for Hungarian and German language. So we had a wonderful time. And then in meantime, we married there. And we stayed there till, uh, '49 till we came here.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to go back and talk a little bit more about when you were in the camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: In what camp? In-- in-- in, uh, Italy? Or in-- in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yugoslavia.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Yugoslavia. What should I tell you? It was horrible. It was just horrible. Because we were so hungry. And we were so afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember one day-- um-- they-- one of the Ustase, his wife was there, who had a little baby. And I remember I came to her and pleaded. I says, can you give me milk? You have a baby. You will understand. You are a mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she kicked me. She says, you Jewish bitch. She says, you don't deserve that. I remember that. And I remember, um, a old lady. She was maybe-- I don't know-- in her 80. And the berg-- they, uh, played football with her. She was rolling. They played football with her. I remember that. Then I remember it was Jewish holiday. And in Saravejo, there was still a Jewish community left. And they send us food. So we-- we-- we got the food-- butter and bread. But they didn't-- with the butter, they were, uh-- they were burning that. So we had to smell. They didn't want to give us this, no bread. They played football with the-- and they were laughing. That I remember. And I know we-- I remember we were sitting all the time in-- in the barracks on-- on the floor. And like I told you, you know, to do the flies, and talking about food, when we're gonna to be liberated, when we go home, how we gonna to meet each other, and what we gonna to cook. We asked recipes from each other, you know. That was our pastime, you know. And some of the girls even think-- we didn't think that was the end, really, that we gonna survive.
